source_texts: We started laughing and joking, we talked without stopping the whole time, I didn't know what I was saying, I was happy. She told me she had seen me one time earlier, a long time ago, in the theater. I was with three friends and had behaved like a lunatic; no question, I had been drunk that time too, that was naughty.

Nana, informed by Labordette of what had taken place, arrived triumphant. She affected the style of a respectable woman, with most distinguished ways, just to astonish every one and to prove to those idiots that, when she liked, not one of them could come up to her; but she almost forgot herself. Rose, as soon as she saw her, flew at her, stammering in a choking voice,
"I? If there is anything in the world I really hate, it is going to bed before twelve o'clock at night!" Oh, is that so!

“What? A priest? It’s not necessary. . . . Where would we get the money to pay him? I have no sins! Even without that God should forgive me. . . . He knows how I’ve suffered! And if He doesn’t forgive me, so be it!”
‘Tonight. All surveillance appears to have vanished from the Nautilus. There seems to be a total stupor on board. Will you be ready?’

The officer’s comrades noticed in that ‘neglected’ garden, behind that sorry-looking rococo gate, quite a pretty creature who was almost always there when the handsome lieutenant went by. This lieutenant is not unknown to the reader: his name was Théodule Gillenormand.

Observing Conseil, I noticed that the good fellow was yielding ever so slightly to the general mood. Or at least I thought so. Perhaps, for the first time in his life, his nerves were tingling with a feeling of curiosity.
Leaning over the railing, he shouted something or other in the direction of the vestibule. The shout was answered with silence, and then, a protesting falsetto:

The hours passed very slowly. It was about half-past eleven when he distinctly heard light, furtive footsteps in the room next to his. Christine was not in her bed, then! Without thinking, Raoul dressed, taking care not to make a sound. And then he waited expectantly. What did he expect? He had no idea. His heart leaped when he heard Christine’s door slowly turn on its hinges. Where could she be going at this hour, when the whole of Perros was asleep? He quietly opened his door and glimpsed her white figure cautiously slipping down the corridor in the moonlight. She reached the staircase and, as she descended, Raoul leaned over the banister. He heard two hurried voices. He only caught one sentence: ‘Don’t lose the key.’ It was the landlady’s voice. Someone opened the door that gave on to the waterfront and locked it again. All went quiet. Raoul rushed back to his room and quickly opened the window. The white figure of Christine stood on the deserted quayside.
She occupied, in the Boulevard Haussmann, the second storey of a large new house, the owner of which was content to let to single ladies, in order to get his plaster dried.
rationales: Outputs 0 and 3 share the same stylistic fingerprint: both use em-dashes, both specify 'three friends/companions', both use 'madman'/'out of my mind' phrasing, both include a flowing emotional narrative style with similar sentence rhythm, and both close with a reflective judgment about the behavior being 'bad.' These linguistic patterns consistently distinguish them from the other outputs.

Outputs 0 and 3 share nearly identical sentence structure and phrasing throughout. Both begin with 'After Labordette told her what had happened, Nana arrived in triumph.' Both use 'put on the manner of a respectable woman' followed by a descriptor of her refined airs. Both use 'simply to amaze everyone and show those [idiots/fools]' with the same syntactic pattern. Both end with 'stammering in a choked voice,' (identical phrasing with a trailing comma). The only differences are minor word substitutions (idiots→fools, forgot herself→gave herself away, distinguished airs→extremely refined ways), which is characteristic of the same paraphrasing system producing slight variations.

Outputs 0 and 4 both omit the 'Me?' opener used in outputs 1 and 2, both reframe the sentiment as a general declaration of strong dislike ('I truly detest'/'despise more than'), and both conclude with a clean 'Oh, really?' response without added exclamations or filler phrases. Their structural template and minimal stylistic choices are consistent with the same paraphraser.

Outputs 0 and 3 share identical stylistic fingerprints: both use curly em-dash quotation marks, the same '. . .' ellipsis style, the same exclamation point placement, nearly identical sentence structures ('What? A priest? There's no need'), and the same closing phrase 'then so be it!' The differences between them are minimal word-level variations (e.g., 'no need for that' vs 'no need', 'afford to pay' vs 'find the money to pay'), which is consistent with slight randomness from the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 1 and 3 share the identical phrase 'Everyone on board seems to be in a complete daze,' which is a very specific linguistic fingerprint suggesting they came from the same paraphraser. Both also follow the same sentence structure and overall flow, differing only slightly in word choices like 'monitoring/observation' and 'disappeared/ceased.'

Outputs 1 and 2 share nearly identical structural patterns: both use 'comrades perceived that there was, in that/this garden' with 'badly kept' in quotation marks, both retain the archaic subordinate clause 'who is not unknown to the reader, and whose name was Théodule Gillenormand,' and both preserve a literal, close-to-source translation style. These shared lexical and syntactic fingerprints strongly suggest they came from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 3 and 4 share nearly identical sentence structure throughout: both open with 'Watching Conseil, I noticed that the good [man/fellow] was giving in [little by little/ever so slightly] to the general mood,' both use a short follow-up sentence ('Or so it seemed to me'/'Or so I believed'), and both close with 'Perhaps, for the first time in his life, his nerves were stirr[ed/ing] with/by a sense of curiosity.' This consistent structural and lexical mirroring across all three sentences strongly indicates the same paraphraser.

Outputs 2 and 3 share the same stylistic fingerprint: both replace 'balustrade' with simpler railing/banister terminology, both describe the voice as 'high-pitched' and 'complaining' (unique descriptors not found in others), both omit 'falsetto' in favor of just 'voice', and both follow the same simplified narrative structure. The word 'complaining' to describe the voice is a distinctive shared choice not present in outputs 0 or 4.

Outputs 1 and 4 share very distinctive linguistic fingerprints: both use 'soft, stealthy footsteps in the room beside his,' 'His heart jumped,' 'creak open on its hinges,' 'all of Perros was asleep,' 'two voices speaking hurriedly/quickly,' the phrase 'Don't lose the key' framed as 'only one phrase/single phrase,' the door to the 'waterfront' being 'locked again,' and 'empty quayside.' These parallel lexical and structural choices strongly indicate they were produced by the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 3 and 4 both use 'owner' (instead of 'landlord') and 'unmarried women' (instead of 'single women'), which are distinctive word choices not shared by outputs 1 or 2. These consistent lexical fingerprints indicate they came from the same paraphrasing system.

I had then comrades with me, and I behaved like a madman; I must certainly have been tipsy that time too, more's the shame.

Apparently I had been with three companions and had acted completely out of my mind; clearly I had been intoxicated on that occasion as well, which was rather bad of me.

human_par3: The hours passed slowly. It must have been half past eleven when he distinctly heard someone moving in the room next to his. The footsteps were light and furtive. Hadn’t Christine gone to bed? Without thinking what he was doing, Raoul dressed quickly, taking care to make no noise. Then, ready for anything, he waited. But ready for what? He had no idea! His heart missed a beat when he heard Christine’s door creak on its hinges. Where could she be going at this time of night when everyone in Perros was asleep? He half opened his door and in a patch of moonlight glimpsed the white figure of Christine gliding cautiously along the corridor. She reached the stairs and went down them. Above her, he leaned over the banister. Suddenly he heard two voices talking urgently. He caught the words: ‘Don’t lose the key.’ It was Mme Tricard’s voice. Then the door overlooking the harbour opened and then closed. Everything went quiet once more. Raoul hurried back to his room, ran to the window and opened it. The white figure of Christine stood on the deserted jetty.
sonnet_46: Time seemed to drag on endlessly. It was around eleven-thirty when Raoul clearly heard soft, stealthy footsteps coming from the room beside his. Christine was not asleep in her room after all! Acting on instinct, he got dressed as quietly as he could and waited, wondering what might happen next. He had no clear idea what to expect. His heart jumped when he heard Christine's door slowly creak open on its hinges. Where could she possibly be heading at this late hour, with all of Perros fast asleep? He gently opened his own door and caught sight of her pale figure carefully making her way along the moonlit corridor. She made her way to the staircase and began to descend, while Raoul leaned over the banister to listen. He could hear two voices speaking hurriedly, but could only make out a single phrase: "Don't lose the key." It was the voice of the landlady. Someone then opened the door leading out to the waterfront, and it was locked again behind them. Silence followed. Raoul hurried back to his room and threw open the window. There on the empty quayside stood Christine's white figure.

Time dragged on. Around 11:30 p.m., Raoul heard soft, stealthy footsteps in the adjacent room and realized Christine was not in her bed. Acting on impulse, he quietly got dressed and waited, though he was unsure what he was waiting for.

His heart pounded when he heard Christine's door creak open. Wondering where she could be going with the entire town asleep, he silently opened his own door. In the moonlight, he spotted her white form moving carefully down the hall. When she started down the staircase, Raoul leaned over the railing to watch. He overheard two quick voices but only managed to catch the landlady saying, "Don't lose the key."

The door leading to the waterfront was opened and then locked, and everything fell silent. Raoul rushed back to his room and opened his window to see Christine's white figure standing alone on the deserted quayside.

She lived on the second floor of a large, newly built apartment building on the Boulevard Haussmann, whose landlord was happy to rent to single women as a way of helping the plaster in the walls dry out.

We burst into laughter and playful banter, chatting nonstop throughout the entire time — I was so caught up in the joy of it that I barely knew what words were coming out of my mouth. She mentioned that she had spotted me once before, quite some time back, at the theater. Apparently I had been with three companions and had acted completely out of my mind; clearly I had been intoxicated on that occasion as well, which was rather bad of me.

After Labordette told her what had happened, Nana arrived in triumph. She put on the manner of a respectable woman, adopting extremely refined ways, simply to amaze everyone and show those fools that, whenever she chose, none of them could match her; but she nearly gave herself away. I? If there was anything in the world I hated it was to go to bed before twelve o'clock at night. Ah, there, you see!

This evening, it appears all observation aboard the Nautilus has ceased. Everyone on board seems to be in a complete daze. Are you prepared?

The handsome lieutenant's comrades took note of a pretty young woman who was frequently in a neglected garden, behind its shabby rococo gate, whenever he went past. The reader is already familiar with this lieutenant, whose name was Théodule Gillenormand.

Watching Conseil, I noticed that the good man was giving in, little by little, to the general mood. Or so it seemed to me. Perhaps, for the first time in his life, his nerves were stirred by a sense of curiosity.
Bending over the banister, he called out something toward the entrance hall. His call was met with quiet, followed by a complaining high-pitched voice:

The hours passed slowly. It must have been half past eleven when he distinctly heard someone moving in the room next to his. The footsteps were light and furtive. Hadn’t Christine gone to bed? Without thinking what he was doing, Raoul dressed quickly, taking care to make no noise. Then, ready for anything, he waited. But ready for what? He had no idea! His heart missed a beat when he heard Christine’s door creak on its hinges. Where could she be going at this time of night when everyone in Perros was asleep? He half opened his door and in a patch of moonlight glimpsed the white figure of Christine gliding cautiously along the corridor. She reached the stairs and went down them. Above her, he leaned over the banister. Suddenly he heard two voices talking urgently. He caught the words: ‘Don’t lose the key.’ It was Mme Tricard’s voice. Then the door overlooking the harbour opened and then closed. Everything went quiet once more. Raoul hurried back to his room, ran to the window and opened it. The white figure of Christine stood on the deserted jetty.
She resided on the second floor of a large, new building on Boulevard Haussmann. The owner was content to rent to unmarried women as a way to help the plaster dry in the recently constructed house.

Our conversation was non-stop, filled with laughter and jokes. I was so overcome with happiness that I wasn't even paying attention to what I was saying. She mentioned that she'd seen me once before, a long time ago at a theater. At the time, I was with three friends and had behaved wildly. I was certainly drunk on that occasion too, which I acknowledged was poor behavior.

Having learned from Labordette what had occurred, Nana entered with a feeling of victory. She intentionally acted like a proper and distinguished lady in order to surprise everyone and prove that, whenever she wanted, none of them could match her. However, she was on the verge of losing her composure. The moment Rose laid eyes on her, she lunged at Nana, her voice choked and stuttering.
"There's nothing in the world I despise more than going to bed before midnight!" "Oh, really?"

The officer's fellow soldiers had observed that in the seemingly untended garden, beyond the shabby rococo gate, there was quite an attractive young woman who could nearly always be spotted whenever the dashing lieutenant passed by. This lieutenant will be familiar to the reader — he was none other than Théodule Gillenormand.

Watching Conseil, I noticed that the good fellow was giving in ever so slightly to the general mood. Or so I believed. Perhaps, for the first time in his life, his nerves were stirring with a sense of curiosity.
He leaned over the railing and shouted some indistinct remark toward the vestibule. Silence answered him at first, followed by a protesting falsetto voice:

Time dragged on. Around half past eleven, he clearly heard soft, stealthy footsteps in the room beside his. So Christine was not in bed! Without pausing to think, Raoul got dressed, careful not to make any noise. Then he waited, tense with anticipation. But what was he expecting? He did not know.

His heart jumped when he heard Christine’s door slowly creak open on its hinges. Where could she be going at such an hour, when all of Perros was asleep? He quietly opened his own door and caught sight of her white figure cautiously moving down the corridor in the moonlight. She reached the stairs, and as she went down, Raoul bent over the banister. He heard two voices speaking quickly. He caught only one phrase: “Don’t lose the key.” It was the landlady speaking. Someone opened the door leading to the waterfront and locked it again. Then everything was silent. Raoul hurried back to his room and quickly opened the window. Christine’s white figure stood on the empty quayside.
She lived on the second floor of a large new building on Boulevard Haussmann, whose owner was willing to rent to unmarried women simply to help dry out the plaster.
